text: During a laparoscopic cholecystectomy using the standard four-port technique, the surgeon grasps the gallbladder and retracts it cranially. Hartman's pouch is retracted upwards and to the right, facilitating the dissection of Calot's triangle. The fat within Calot's triangle is meticulously removed to prevent bleeding, and the cystic duct is identified and separated from the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and hook, along with diathermy, to dissect strands of areolar tissue, providing a clear view of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and the base of the gallbladder. The critical view of safety is achieved. A clip is applied to the lower end of Hartman's pouch, followed by a small incision to introduce the cholangiogram catheter. The catheter is flushed with saline and contrast, revealing normal anatomy without common bile duct stones.
The surgeon clips and cuts the cystic duct and cystic artery, completing the devascularization of the gallbladder.

The surgeon continues the cranial dissection, carefully separating the gallbladder from the liver bed. Attention is given to identifying any abnormal ducts, such as the ducts of Luschka, while avoiding perforation of the thin-walled gallbladder and preventing bile leakage or hemorrhage.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder and applies appropriate tension to expose the important dissection plane. A hook is used to carefully dissect the tissue while ensuring excellent hemostasis.

The surgeon inserts a BERT bag into the abdominal cavity and places the gallbladder within it to minimize wound infection risk during extraction. The gallbladder is extracted via the umbilical port using a ten millimeter grasper. A washout is performed, showing clear fluid without bile or blood, which helps reduce post-operative pain. The camera is repositioned to the epigastric port for better visualization during extraction.
